summary: Jeff Atwood challenges the ubiquitous startup advice to 'only hire the best,' arguing that this approach is both impractical and harmful. He points out that most startups fail regardless of hiring quality due to factors beyond their control, making the obsessive pursuit of top talent less critical than commonly believed. More importantly, he argues that ultra-selective hiring processes that reject candidates at the slightest doubt inadvertently optimize for implicit bias, favoring 'People Like Us' and undermining diversity. Atwood advocates for alternative approaches like audition projects (paid trial work periods), geographic diversity through remote work, and expanding hiring horizons beyond Silicon Valley's standard playbook.
key_points: The 'hire only the best' advice ignores that most startups fail due to product-market fit issues, not talent quality
Ultra-selective hiring that rejects candidates at any doubt accidentally optimizes for implicit bias and 'People Like Us' preferences
Research shows unconscious bias affects decisions significantly—from baseball card sales to orchestra auditions to police shootings
Audition projects (paid trial work) are more effective than traditional interviews because you actually work with candidates on real tasks
Geographic diversity through remote work can help achieve better overall diversity by not limiting hiring to a 30-mile radius of San Francisco
money_quotes: It doesn't matter how good the people are at your company when you happen to be working on the wrong problem, at the wrong time, using the wrong approach.

If your hiring attitude is that it's better to be possibly wrong a hundred times so you can be absolutely right one time, you're going to be primed to throw away a lot of candidates on pretty thin evidence.
When you do, you are accidentally optimizing for implicit bias.
I don't think I am one of the best. More like cranky, tenacious, and outspoken, to the point that I wake up most days not even wanting to work with myself.
If you want better diversity in the field, perhaps a good starting point is not demanding that all your employees live within a tiny 30 mile radius of San Francisco or Palo Alto.
Interviews, no matter how much effort you put into them, are so hit and miss that the only way to figure out if someone is really going to work in a given position is to actually work with them.
Every company says they want to hire the best. Anyone who tells you they know how to do that is either lying to you or to themselves.
key_insight: The industry's obsession with hiring 'only the best' through highly selective interview processes inadvertently optimizes for implicit bias and homogeneity, and companies should instead embrace audition projects and geographic diversity to build better teams.
